text: Concurrent resistance and endurance training may attenuate gains in muscle mass, strength, and power compared with resistance training alone.
Endurance exercise can activate molecular signaling responses in skeletal muscle that inhibit protein synthesis pathways and stimulate protein breakdown.
Human studies have not yet observed molecular interference following acute concurrent exercise sufficient to explain compromised muscle hypertrophy from concurrent training.
Key training variables that may modulate the concurrent interference effect include within-session exercise order, between-mode recovery time, endurance training volume, intensity, and modality.
Despite potential interference effects, concurrent training offers additive benefits for disease prevention and athletic performance by combining resistance and endurance adaptations.
